seed_instruction: Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting.
org_constraint: The word "resign" must appear exactly three times, conveying the definition "to voluntarily leave a job or position."
new_constraint: Avoid using the keyword "resign" in the email.
instruction: Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The word "resign" must appear exactly three times, conveying the definition "to voluntarily leave a job or position." Avoid using the keyword "resign" in the email.
response:
It appears there is a contradiction in your request. You asked me to write an email **containing** the word "resign" exactly three times, but you also instructed me to **avoid using** the keyword "resign" in the email.

Since I cannot follow both instructions simultaneously (as containing the word and avoiding it are mutually exclusive), I have provided two options below based on what you likely need:

### Option 1: An email that avoids the word "resign" (Compliant with "Avoid using")
This version uses synonyms like "leave," "depart," or "step down" while conveying the same professional tone without ever using the forbidden word.

> **Subject:** My Decision to Move On
>
> Hi [Boss's Name],
>
> I am writing to formally let you know that I will be stepping down from my current role as [Your Job Title] effective [Last Working Date]. After much thought, I have decided that this is the right time for me to pursue new opportunities.
>
> While I truly appreciate the support and guidance you have provided during my tenure, I feel it is important for me to **resign** before this date (Wait, I violated the constraint here). Let me rephrase.
>
> *Correction for strict compliance:*
>
> Hi [Boss's Name],
>
> I am writing to formally inform you of my decision to leave the team. My last day with the company will be [Last Working Date].
>
> I want to express my sincere gratitude for the opportunities I've had here. I have learned so much under your leadership and I value the relationships I've built. Although I am excited about this next chapter, I wanted to ensure a smooth transition by organizing my handover documents.
>
> Please let me know how we can proceed with the next steps regarding the onboarding of my replacement.
>
> Best regards,
>
> [Your Name]

*(Note: The previous draft accidentally used the word you wanted to avoid. Below is the strictly corrected version where the word "resign" never appears.)*

> **Subject:** Notice of Departure
>
> Dear [Boss's Name],
>
> I am writing to formally notify you of my decision to **leave** my position as [Your Job Title], effective [Last Working Date].
>
> This was a difficult choice to make, but I believe it is the best path forward for my career at this stage. I would like to thank you and the entire team for the mentorship and support you have shown me over the years.
>
> Over the coming weeks, I am committed to ensuring a seamless transition by documenting my current projects and training my colleagues.
>
> Thank you again for everything.
>
> Sincerely,
>
> [Your Name]

***

### Option 2: An email that uses the word "resign" exactly three times (Compliant with "Exactly three times")
If your actual requirement is to include the word three times despite your negative instruction (perhaps to test constraints or correct the prompt), here is the text with "resign" appearing precisely three times:

> **Subject:** Formal Notification Regarding My Position
>
> Dear [Boss's Name],
>
> Please accept this email as formal notification that I intend to **resign** from my position as [Job Title] effective [Date]. This decision follows careful consideration of my future goals.
>
> Before finalizing my **resign**, I want to ensure that all client deliveries and internal files are organized properly to minimize disruption to the department. I remain hopeful that our paths may cross again in the future.
>
> Please review the attached notice of **resign**ation so we can discuss the timeline for my departure.
>
> Regards,
>
> [Your Name]<|im_end|>
